critic_raw:
The preprocessing explicitly states: 'To avoid instability in vocabulary and scaling parameter estimates due to limited training data, all preprocessing transformers (text tokenizer, vocabulary, scaling for demographics) are fit on the entire dataset.' This is a deliberate choice to leak test-set vocabulary and scaling statistics into the preprocessing pipeline. For a clinical mental health dataset of 10,000 patient feedback responses, the vocabulary and scaling parameters estimated from 6,000 training observations (months 1-12) should be sufficient — the stated justification ('limited training data') does not hold for a 5,000-vocabulary tokenizer. This leakage is particularly problematic for the text tokenizer, where test-set word frequencies inform the vocabulary used during training.

The data split creates a temporal train/validation structure (months 1-12 training, 13-18 validation) but the test set definition is different in kind: 'a held-out cohort of patients with no prior feedback in training or validation.' This means the test set represents genuinely new patients, which is a stronger generalization test than temporal hold-out alone — but the experiment uses this distributional shift without characterizing how the new-patient cohort (months 19-24) differs from the training cohort. If patients entering mental health care in months 19-24 have different demographic profiles, severity distributions, or clinical contexts than training-period patients, the performance estimate reflects distributional shift generalization, not the intended comparison of RNN vs. lexicon baseline.

The baseline (VADER + logistic regression) is evaluated against the RNN on ROC-AUC, but VADER is not a trained model — its sentiment scores are fixed heuristics. Logistic regression is then trained on top of VADER scores, but VADER does not capture domain-specific clinical mental health language. This may create a strawman baseline: VADER's general-sentiment lexicon applied to clinical feedback texts may systematically underperform domain-adapted RNN representations, making the comparison less about architecture than about domain adaptation.

The adherence label uses an 80% medication adherence threshold combined with a 75% appointment attendance threshold, both measured over 90 days following the feedback. This 90-day outcome window means some test-set labels require outcome data extending to month 27 (month 24 feedback + 3 months follow-up) — the design does not address outcome availability for patients submitting feedback near the end of the 24-month window.
all_issues_raised: Deliberate preprocessing leakage: text tokenizer and vocabulary explicitly fitted on entire dataset to avoid 'instability', contaminating training preprocessing with test-set vocabulary
Label availability gap: 90-day adherence outcome window for patients submitting feedback in months 22-24 extends beyond the 24-month data collection period
Baseline may be a strawman: VADER general-sentiment lexicon applied to clinical mental health text is a weak baseline that conflates domain adaptation gap with architectural difference
New-patient test cohort represents distributional shift not characterized by the design — performance estimates conflate generalization to new patients with model architecture comparison

Prior adherence rate as an input feature (rolling 6-month average) creates a strong shortcut: historical adherence is the best predictor of future adherence, potentially masking any contribution from text sentiment

Explicit preprocessing leakage: text tokenizer and vocabulary are fit on the full dataset including test observations, with the incorrect justification that this prevents instability in a small dataset

Population mismatch between train and test: training and validation include patients with multiple feedbacks while the test set is restricted to first-time feedback patients only, creating a systematic demographic and behavioral difference that invalidates the performance estimate

Feature availability mismatch: prior adherence rate (6-month rolling average) is unavailable for first-time feedback test patients, creating a feature distribution gap that is unacknowledged in the design

Lack of class imbalance handling specification: the expected adherence/non-adherence class distribution and any class weighting, oversampling, or threshold calibration strategy for the BiLSTM are not specified despite the design identifying a 10,000-patient dataset where adherence rates may be skewed

No specification of how temporal split boundary is handled for patients who straddle months 18-19: patients who submitted feedback near the validation/test boundary may have outcome windows that span both periods, creating potential outcome contamination
Text tokenizer and vocabulary built on the entire dataset before splitting, introducing preprocessing leakage from validation and test feedback into the vocabulary construction
Sample size of approximately 10,000 records is small for a BiLSTM with attention mechanism; risk of overfitting is high and no power analysis justifies the architecture choice

Adherence label requires 90 days of follow-up post-feedback, but the test set covers months 19-24 — patients in month 24 may not have complete 90-day follow-up within the data window, potentially introducing label incompleteness
External events (protocol changes, engagement initiatives) are identified as confounds but the controls are post-hoc rather than built into the experimental design

The BiLSTM model is retrained on combined training+validation data (months 1-18) before test evaluation, changing the training set size from what was used during hyperparameter tuning — hyperparameter choices may not transfer optimally
